summary: Jeff Atwood criticizes the persistent 'Do you want to save?' dialog that applications still present to users, referencing Alan Cooper's 1995 book 'About Face' which argued against this pattern a decade earlier. He argues that with modern hardware capabilities like large hard drives, applications should automatically save everything and allow users to undo later if needed. The post highlights the frustrating lack of progress in UI design and contrasts traditional applications with database applications that save automatically, which users find surprisingly intuitive.
key_points: The 'save changes' dialog is a decades-old UI anti-pattern that persists despite being criticized since 1995
Modern hardware capabilities (large storage) eliminate the technical excuses for not auto-saving
Applications should save automatically and let users undo, rather than asking permission upfront
Database applications already implement auto-save, proving the pattern works and users prefer it
Difficulty of implementation is not a valid excuse for poor user experience
money_quotes: This rationale is like saying that a beaten dog expects to be beaten again, so we should beat it to make it happy.
The time to make our programs better is now.
In this era of 300gb hard drives, I should never be explicitly prompted for much of anything.
Save it all, and recall the stuff I need. Don't ask me for permission, just do it, and let me take it back later if I need to.
Of course this is harder to program, but so what?
When presented with a database application for the first time, many users ask: 'But how do I save?' Imagine that. An application that saves your work automatically. Revolutionary!
key_insight: Applications should embrace automatic saving and undo rather than burdening users with save dialogs—the difficulty of implementation is not an excuse for poor UX.
